passages.explanation: Jesus tells a parable to his disciples: a rich man's manager is accused of wasting his possessions. The master calls him in: what's this I hear about you? Give an account of your management — you're fired. The manager thinks: what shall I do? I'm not strong enough to dig, and I'm ashamed to beg. I know what I'll do so that when I lose my job, people will welcome me. He calls each of his master's debtors. The first owes a hundred measures of olive oil — he tells him to sit down and change it to fifty. The second owes a hundred measures of wheat — make it eighty. The master commends the dishonest manager for acting shrewdly. Jesus says: the people of this world are more shrewd in dealing with their own kind than the people of the light. Use worldly wealth to gain friends, so that when it's gone, you'll be welcomed into eternal dwellings. The parable is deliberately provocative — Jesus isn't praising dishonesty. He's praising the manager's urgency and strategic use of resources when facing a deadline. If a crook can plan that well for his temporary future, how much more should believers invest in eternal relationships?

Whoever can be trusted with very little can also be trusted with much. Whoever is dishonest with very little will also be dishonest with much. If you haven't been trustworthy with worldly wealth, who will trust you with true riches? If you haven't been trustworthy with someone else's property, who will give you property of your own? No servant can serve two masters — either you'll hate one and love the other, or be devoted to one and despise the other. You can't serve both God and money. The Pharisees, who love money, hear all this and sneer at Jesus. He says: you justify yourselves in the eyes of others, but God knows your hearts. What people value highly is detestable in God's sight. The Pharisees' reaction proves the point — their contempt for Jesus' teaching about money reveals which master they actually serve.

The Law and the Prophets were proclaimed until John. Since that time, the good news of the kingdom of God is being preached, and everyone is forcing their way into it. But it's easier for heaven and earth to disappear than for the least stroke of a pen to drop out of the law. Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another woman commits adultery, and the man who marries a divorced woman commits adultery. Jesus affirms the law's permanence while announcing the kingdom's arrival. The divorce statement isn't random — it illustrates the principle. The Pharisees found loopholes in the law; Jesus says the law's moral demands haven't changed just because the kingdom has come.

Jesus tells the story of a rich man who dresses in purple and fine linen and lives in luxury every day. At his gate lies a beggar named Lazarus, covered with sores, longing to eat what falls from the rich man's table. Dogs come and lick his sores. The beggar dies and is carried by the angels to Abraham's side. The rich man also dies and is buried. In Hades, where he's in torment, he looks up and sees Abraham far away, with Lazarus by his side. He calls out: Father Abraham, have pity on me and send Lazarus to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue — I'm in agony in this fire. Abraham replies: son, remember that in your lifetime you received your good things while Lazarus received bad things. Now he's comforted here and you're in agony. Besides, a great chasm has been set between us — no one can cross from either side. The rich man begs: then send Lazarus to my father's house — I have five brothers. Let him warn them so they don't come to this place of torment. Abraham says: they have Moses and the Prophets — let them listen to them. The rich man insists: no, Father Abraham, but if someone from the dead goes to them, they will repent. Abraham replies: if they don't listen to Moses and the Prophets, they won't be convinced even if someone rises from the dead. The rich man's sin isn't being rich — it's being indifferent to suffering at his own gate. And the final line is prophetic: Jesus himself will rise from the dead, and many still won't believe.
